text: Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 1 Normal Systems Notes Semester 2, 2010 Musculoskeletal System Toby Dawson (20517564) Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 2 Part 1: Anatomy Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 3 Lecture 1: Introduction to Limb Plan and Development Musculoskeletal Anatomy  Bones form joints, which are moved by muscles, which are controlled by nerves Limbs Overview  Upper and lower limbs built to a common plan  Developmentally they are outgrowths of the body wall and as such the segmental pattern of the body wall is evident Developmental Origins  Humans begin as a tri-laminar embryo: Ectoderm->Mesoderm->Endoderm  Mesoderm can be divided into paraxial, intermediate and lateral plate divisions.  The paraxial mesoderm undergoes segmentation to form blocks called somites.  Each somite has sclerotome (bone) and dermomyotome (skin and muscle).  Dermatome forms the dermis of a body segment.  Myotome forms the skeletal muscle of a segment.  Pattern seen in thoracic and abdominal wall is that dermatome and myotome “migrate”. Nerves travel with muscle (myotome).  Number of segments: o Neck – 8 o Thorax – 12 o Lumbar Region – 5 o Sacral Region – 5 o (4 coccygeal) o Each segment has its nerve (and number)  Limb segments: o Upper Limb : C5 – T1 o Lower Limb: L2 – S3 o They are lateral outgrowths, the limbs “rotate” later. Paraxial Mesoderm Somite Sclerotome + Dermomyotome Dermatome + Myotome Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 4 Limb Plan  Axial skeleton has a girdle (half ring on one side) attached.  Single bone attaches to girdle (arm/thigh).  Two bones attach to single bone (forearm/leg)  Then set of small bones (carpus/tarsus)  Then metacarpals/metatarsals  Then phalanges (digits and thumb/great toe) Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 5 Lecture 2: Bone - Structure, Development and Growth Skeletal Tissues  Connective tissue o Contains cells and intercellular matrix o Matrix (non-cellular material produced by the cells of the CT) consists of:  Fibres  Collagen (most abundant, occurs in bundles)  Elastic (can stretch and recoil)  Reticular (very fine and form a delicate network)  Ground substance  Complex macromolecules Fibrous Connective Tissue  Collagen o Resists stretching o May exist in an irregular arrangement (fibres in various directions). Irregularity is apparent because collagen fibres are laid down wherever a tissue is subject to force. o There are also regular “parallel” bundles. Usually seen as cords (tendons).  Fibrous tissue does not resist compression Cartilage  Has the same functions of collagen and also resists compression, due to the properties of its ground substance  Cells in “lacunae” (“hole” in the matrix) in a histological section o Chrondroblasts and chondrocytes  2 important varieties 1. Hyaline cartilage has a “glassy” appearance (contains collagen and elastic fibres) 2. White Fibrocartilage has a large proportion of collagen, making it slightly deformable/compressible (found in the vertebral column)  Cartilage is avascular, blood vessels are around not within  Perichondrium is a covering that lines some cartilages and contains blood vessels, allows exchange of nutrients  Growth of cartilage may be: o From perichondrium: Apposition o From within: Interstitial growth Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 6 Bone  Has a mineralised matrix (calcium), which has an orderly deposition along collagen fibres. Calcium matrix is in the form of microcrystals of calcium hyrdoxyapatite.  Bone can resist stretching, compression and shearing (twisting) forces.  Bone comprises of layers (lamellae) with collagen in different directions.  Each layer has its cells o Resting: Osteocytes o Active: Osteoblasts  Bone is highly vascularised (the hard matrix makes diffusion impossible, and so this is necessary for nutrient delivery) Gross Forms  Compact Bone  Trabecular bone (aka cancellous or spongy)  Hollow structure arrangement gives economy of mass. Also allows for a medullary cavity which may contain bone forming or fatty marrow. Development  Bone is of mesodermal origin  Undifferentiated mesodermal cells undergo differentiation to become osteoblasts (with the right stimulation) and will then start producing bone substances  Growth is limited is that it can only undergo appositional growth (no interstitial growth, unlike cartilage)  Flat bones (eg. of skull) develop in mesodermal membrane  All other bones develop from intermediate cartilage  Intramembranous Ossification o Process of undifferentiated cells Osteoblasts o Osteoblasts produce the matrix which surrounds the cells. The cells are the “ossification centre” o At birth  Unossified tissue: fibrous joint  Fontanelles exist between flat bones of the skull  This allows for moulding of the foetal head under pressure from the birth canal during birth. Flat bones may override each other (slid over each other).  Later these become sutures between the flat bones  Endochondrial Ossification o Long Bone Histologically similar T C Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 7  Consists of a hollow shaft with two ends  Long bones begin as a cartilage model which undergoes endochondrial ossification  Cartilage is replaced by bone (not converted) o Process of endochondrial ossification 1. Primary centre before birth 2. Cartilage dies and is replaced. The dead cartilage acts as a template/scaffolding for the bone laid down. 3. At birth the shaft is ossified and still growing 4. After birth secondary centres, the epiphyseal plate allows for growth. The growth mechanism is shown in the diagram below. Last epiphyseal plate cartilage dies between the ages of 18-25 and growth can no longer occur (epiphyseal union) Summary  Bone is living and dynamic  It responds to forces o Muscles, tendons, ligaments o Tuberosities, ridges, depressions  It develops around structures o Foramina, canals  Constantly undergoes repair and remodelling Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 8 Lecture 6: Joints and Muscles Joints  Refers to where two or more bones, or parts of bones unite, and encompasses the tissues that unite the bones. Joint Classification  Structural o The tissues that unite bones  fibrous tissue, cartilage or fibrous tissue with a cavity  Functional o Mobility or otherwise Fibrous Joints  Sutures (stitches or stitch lines) o Between flat bones of the skull o Unossified areas between membrane bones, these tend to disappear as ossification proceeds  Other fibrous joints o Syndesmoses (of ligaments) which are fibrous bands or sheets of fibrous tissue between bones. Eg. Interosseous membranes (forearm and leg), inferior tibio-fibular joint Cartilaginous Joints  Synchondrosis  A joint in which parts are joined by hyaline cartilage. This makes the joint functionally a growth mechanism o Growth mechanism  Helps form a “synostosis” which means two bones are united to form one bone. A synostosis can form where ligaments closely approximate two bones, and the ligament calcifies to form a bony joint. o Exist between primary and secondary centres in long bones, and between named bones (eg. sphenoidal and occipital). In long bones a plate of hyaline cartilage is replaced by bone when growth is complete.  Symphysis  White fibrocartilage o White fibrocartilage is deformable as it has lots of collagen, this functionally means joints are partially movable Synovial joints  Most movable type of joint  A fibrous tissue bond with a cavity  The bones are the articular surfaces and are covered in articular cartilage  Joint is surrounded by a fibrous capsule (a cylindrical column of fibrous tissue)  May also contain (extrinsic) ligaments which function to add strength and thicken the capsule Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 9  Inside the joint is a synovial membrane (epithelial tissue, 2-4 cell layers, supported by loose connective tissue), which lines the capsule (except the articular surfaces). It secretes synovial fluid (similar to egg white) which lubricates the joint.  An additional feature of some joints is that they may contain discs of white fibrocartilage (intra-articular disc), which helps to reduce mismatch between bones. Also may contain other white fibrocartilage structures (eg. menisci), (intra-articular) ligaments (additional ligaments inside the cavity), tendons passing through (eg. long head of biceps tendon) and pads of fat.  Subtypes: o Functional  Different degrees of freedom (movability) o Structural  Shapes of bony surfaces may be flat, forming a hinge, ball and socket, etc. Bursae  Friction reducing thin walled connective tissue bags lined by synovial membrane (containing synovial fluid). Are important because muscles and tendons in close proximity to a joint can be subject to a lot of friction.  Also between: o Joint capsule and tendon/muscle o Bone and tendon/muscle o Muscles/tendons o Skin and bone (eg. between olecranon of elbow and skin  “students elbow”)  Clinically bursae are important because they may become painfully inflamed (bursitis), and some communicate with joint cavities Joint Stability  There is a compromise between movement and stability  Factors in stability are: o Bony configuration o Capsule and ligaments o Tendons and muscles (sometimes blend with capsule  rotator cuff) Nerve Supply  Rich sensory supply around joints  Supply the capsule, ligaments and periosteum  Same nerves as those that supply muscles Blood Supply  Vessels go around joints Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 10  In the limbs there are lots of anastomoses around joints as blood supply may be compromised during movement  Note that hyaline cartilage is avascular Skeletal Muscles  Attach to at least two bones and bring about movement at a joint  Movements include flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, rotation (lateral/medial)  A muscle may have more than one action at a joint, and a muscle may act on more than one joint (a muscle acts on all joints it crosses)  “Prime movers” are the main muscle(s) responsible for a movement. Synergist muscles supplement the prime mover and help to neutralise unwanted actions of the prime mover. Antagonists exert gentle control on the prime mover, bringing about termination of movement. Muscles are called fixators when the prime mover and antagonist act together to form a stiff base for other muscles to act.  Clinically you must consider that muscle weakness/paralysis may be the result of muscle disease or nerve injury Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 11 Lecture 9: Shoulder Joint and Girdle Joint and Girdle  The shoulder joint is more correctly the “scapulohumeral joint”  It exhibits a great range of movement  Shoulder (pectoral) girdle consists of the scapula and clavicle and has two joints: o Sternoclavicular joint: scapula moves with the clavicle o Acromioclavicular joint: very little movement  Functionally the shoulder girdle and the scapulohumeral joints form a single unit Scapulohumeral Joint  Scapula: contains the glenoid cavity, which is a shallow socket. It is slightly deepened by labrum (made of white fibrocartilage)  Humerus: The head is approximately the shape of half a ball  The joint is highly movable but unstable  In the “dependent” (resting) position the capsule is stretched at the top and loose inferiorly  Glenohumeral ligaments (thickened areas of the capsule itself) provide anterior thickening  The coracoacromial ligament and arch provide protection superiorly (prevents upward movement)  Most strength and stability of the joint comes from muscles close to the joint: o The tendons of the muscles blend with the capsule o Muscles of the rotator cuff provide dynamic supports to the joint. They have a differing ability to resist stretching depending on the activity of muscles. All 4 of the muscle converge to the head of the humerus and the tendons of each form a tight ring around the shoulder joint, joining with the capsule. All of the muscles (except supraspinatus) are rotators of the shoulder, and they form a cuff, hence the name “rotator cuff” muscles. The muscles are:  Supraspinatus  Infraspinatus  Teres minor  Subscapularis Supraspinatus Subscapularis Infraspinatus Teres minor Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 12 Stability of a Joint  Greater mobility leads to less stability for a joint  Stability depends on: o Bony factors (bone shape  fitting) o Capsule and ligaments (ligaments are static resistors and so may affect movement. For lots of movement muscles are used for support in the shoulder, see below) o Muscles around the joint (allows more dynamic stability, rotator cuff muscles of the shoulder are a good example)  The downside of the great mobility of the shoulder joint is that the looseness of the capsule means it is prone to injury/tear. Dislocation of the joint inferiorly is common. Wear and tear may occur in the rotator cuff and subacromial bursa, supraspinatus tendon, and may cause greater tuberosity impingement. Movements  Flexion/extension: horizontal coronal axis  Abduction/adduction: horizontal sagittal axis o Abduction  Away from midline o Adduction  Towards midline  Rotation: medial or lateral  vertical axis, through the shaft of humerus Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 13 Girdle Movements  The clavicle moves with the scapula  Mainly “rotation” of the scapula. In upward rotation the glenoid faces up, in downward rotation the glenoid faces down. The muscles which achieve this are attaches to the trunk  Protraction (pulling the medial border of the scapula forward) is achieved by the serratus anterior Muscles and Movements  To move the scapulohumeral joint to distal attachment of muscles is on the humerus and the proximal attachment is on the trunk or scapula  Some muscles are more important as stabilizers, rather than for movement  Pectoralis major: o Flexion and adduction  Latissimus dorsi: o Extension and adduction  Other flexors are any muscles anterior to the shoulder (eg. subscapularis, part of deltoid)  Other extensors are any muscles posterior to the shoulder (eg. infraspinatus, part of deltoid)  Abduction: o Deltoid is the large abductor, however in early stages the supraspinatus causes the movement o The middle part of the deltoid is the strongest and thickest. Anterior and posterior parts are thin, parallel bundles and do not play a role in abduction o Abduction is limited by bone structures Together these cause adduction Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 14  Rotation: o Rotational movement depends on where the muscles attach on the humerus o Anterior attachments produce medial rotation. Muscles include:  Pectoralis Major, Latissimus Dorsi, Teres Major, Subscapularis o Posterior attachments produce lateral rotation. Muscles include:  Infraspinatus, Teres minor Rotator Cuff Muscles  Their role in stability is most important, however other roles include: o Infraspinatus being the only lateral rotator o Subscapularis being a fairly strong medial rotator  Medial rotation is the stronger movement o Supraspinatus plays an important role in initiating abduction (first 15 degrees of movement) Other Muscles  Biceps Brachii: o Contributes to stability of shoulder joint (rather than mainly being used for movement) o Long and short heads attach to the scapula: Short head to the coracoid, long head to the supraglenoid tubercle. The long head is intracapsular, which contributes to stability of the joint.  Triceps Brachii: o Again useful as a stabiliser o The long head attaches to the scapula Bursae of Shoulder Joint  Can be numerous  One constant (in all/most people) include: o Subacromial (aka subdeltoid)  Subject to injury o Subscapular (may be continuous with synovial membrane)  Other bursae exist between various muscles and tendons Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 15 Anatomy Lab 1: Pectoral and Scapular Regions Task 1: Osteology  Recognise the bones of the upper limb, and their basic features Clavicle  Medial end is broad  Lateral end is flattened  Upper surface is smooth  Lower surface is rough  Lateral part (approx. 1/3) is concave forwards, remaining 2/3 (medial) is convex forward  Medial end forms the sternoclavicular joint  Medial end is just above the anterior end of the first rib, connected by the costoclavicular ligament (hence medial rough area of clavicle)  Lateral end forms a joint with the scapula Scapula  The oval glenoid fossa at the lateral angle forms the joint with the humerus Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 16  The coracoid process is close to the glenoid (looks like a bent thumb)  The long spine projects like a shelf from the posterior surface  Spine broadens into the acromion process laterally  Spine is closer to the superior border and separates two fossae (depressions): 1. Supraspinous fossa (above) 2. Infrspinous fossa (below)  The anterior surface is a great hollow and called the subscapular fossa  The scapula lies approximately midway between the coronal and sagittal planes  Acromion (tip off shoulder) is above the head of the humerus  Coracoid process is below the lateral rough impression on the clavicle. Coracoclavicular ligament connects the two  The scapula does not form a joint with any bone of the axial skeleton. The upper limb is connected to the axial skeleton only through the sternoclavicular joint Humerus  Upper end: o Articular are has a round head (half sphere surface) o Two tuberosities Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 17  Greater tuberosity  Lesser tuberosity (faces anteriorly)  Groove in between (faces anteriorly) o Margin of the smooth head is called the anatomical neck o Head and tuberosities develop from second secondary centres, which merge with each other before they unite with the shaft  Lower end: o Articular area has a small, round head-like part called the capitulum which is lateral, and the trochlear which is medial o Bony projections called epicondyles exist above the capitulum and trochlear. Larger epicondyle is medial, smaller one is lateral. Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 18 Task 2: Muscles: Deltoid, pectoral muscle and serratus anterior. Deltoid  Covers the shoulder joint on 3 sides, giving the region a round shape  Origin of muscle is the clavicle, acromion and spine  Fibres converge to a small area at insertion onto a rough area in the middle (lateral side) of the shaft of the humerus (deltoid tuberosity)  Middle portion of the deltoid (“multipennate”) arising from the acromion is thick, anterior and posterior parts are thinner. Principle action of deltoid is due to middle thick part Pectoralis Major  Broad muscle with two distinct parts (with small gap between the two) o One from the sternum and ribs o Other from clavicle  Attachment at humerus. Muscle passes laterally and backwards towards the humerus, and so pulls humerus forwards and medially. Pectoralis Minor  Reflected underneath Pec. Major.  Origin from coracoid  Attaches to ribs 3, 4, 5 Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 19 Subclavius  Small muscle in a groove on the under surface of the clavicle. It steadies the clavicle. Serratus Anterior  On lateral side of thoracic wall  Has multiple “slips” attached to the ribs  Inserts on medial border of scapula (coracoid process)  Pulls medial border of scapula (and so entire scapula) forward towards the costal attachment of the muscle. i.e. is a protractor of the scapula. Performed when pushing something with the outstretched limb.  Its nerve may be damaged by a chest drain or mastectomy Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 20 Task 3: Muscles: Back and Scapular Region Trapezius  Large triangular muscle arises from skull, cervical and half the thoracic vertebrae  Fibres converge to part of the clavicle, and spine and acromion of scapula  Upper part elevates scapula and clavicle (shrugging)  Middle part draws scapula towards the vertebral column  Upper and lower parts together rotate the scapula Rhomboid  Parallelogram shaped muscle under the cover of the trapezius Latissimus Dorsi  Wide origin from part of the hip bone, the thoracolumbar fascia and lower thoracic vertebrae  Muscle “spirals” as it passes the scapula  Muscle inserts on the humerus, close to the pectoralis major  Pulls the humerus medially and backwards Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 21 Muscles Around the Shoulder  Supraspinatus o Above the spine of scapula (on posterior surface)  Infraspinatus o Below spine of scapula (on posterior surface)  Subscapularis o Under cover of the scapula, facing the ribs. Occupies almost the entire subscapular fossa.  Teres Major  Teres Minor Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 22 Lecture 3: Axilla and Brachial Plexus Axilla  A “pyramidal” space  Apex is a narrow inlet also known as the cervico-axillary canal. Through this space structures enter and exit the upper limbs. The apex is bounded by the clavicle anteriorly, scapula posteriorly and first rib as a curved boundary medially  Has anterior and posterior “axillary folds” o Anterior: Pectoralis major fold of skin o Posterior: Latissimus Dorsi fold of skin Contents  Part of brachial plexus  Axillary artery and vein  Lymph nodes: All lymph from the upper limbs is drained by the axillary lymph nodes. They also drain lymph from the front side of the axilla, underneath the pectoralis major, and they also drain a large portion of the thoracic and abdominal wall (above the level of the umbilicus). On the posterior side of the anterior wall they also drain part of the mammary glands.  Fat (in which lymph nodes are found) Spinal Nerves: Pattern  Spinal nerves emerge from vertebral canals. As soon as it exits it divides into two main branches (primary rami): o Dorsal (anterior) o Ventral (posterior)  The dorsal rami has a limited distribution (through the length of the vertebral column they mainly supply the muscles posterior to the vertebral column and small patches of skin)  The ventral rami have a wider distribution. Certain ventral rami make up the brachial plexus. Brachial Plexus  Made of ventral rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1  C5-C8 come from the neck, T1 from the thorax. This means that part of the plexus exists outside the upper limb (neck, thorax and axilla)  Undergoes stages of branching and reunion, final branches are named Thoracic wall (ribs) 1 of 3 cords of brachial plexus Axillary artery and vein Pectoralis Major Latissimus Dorsi Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 23 Brachial Plexus Scheme  Consists of 5 roots, 3 trunks, 6 divisions and 3 cords  Roots combine to form upper (superior), middle and lower (inferior) trunks. The trunks divide into anterior and posterior divisions. The anterior and posterior divisions reunite in a definite pattern. Anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks unite to form the lateral cord. The anterior division of the lower trunk continues to form the medial cord. All 3 posterior divisions unite to form the posterior cord. These cords of the brachial plexus are where most branches of nerves to the upper limb come from. Note that the lateral and medial cords are not only anterior to the axillary artery, but they also come from anterior divisions. Also note that the posterior cord is not just posterior to the axillary artery, but it also comes from posterior divisions.  Roots and trunks lie above the clavicle  Divisions lie behind the clavicle  Cords and branches lie below the clavicle (in the axilla)  Important points in understanding the scheme: o Divisions are crucial to understanding o Each trunk has two divisions: anterior and posterior o Considering the muscles: Anterior divisions go to anterior compartment muscles (mainly flexors). Posterior divisions go to posterior compartment muscles (mainly extensors). o Posterior divisions  Posterior cord ( Posterior compartment muscles) o Anterior divisions  Lateral and medial cords( Anterior compartment muscles) Posterior Cord Branches  Axillary nerve Deltoid (and teres minor)  Nerves to latissimus dorsi (and subscapularis)  Radial nerve  Extensor compartments of arm and forearm Clavicle To latissimus dorsi Axillary nerve To subscapularis (x3) Radial nerve Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 24 Lateral Cord Branches  Musculocutaneous nerve  Anterior compartment of arm (flexors) Medial Cord Branches  Ulnar nerve  Flexor compartment of forearm and small muscles in the hand Lateral and Medial Cord (Uniting) Branch  Median nerve  Flexor compartment of forearm and small muscles in the hand Summary of Arm Muscle Compartments and Nerves  1 nerve to posterior (extensor) compartments of arm and forearm = Radial nerve  1 nerve to anterior (flexor) compartment of arm = Musculocutaneous nerve  2 nerves to anterior (flexor) compartments of forearm = Median and Ulnar nerves Nerves Root Value  Able to be determined by understanding the brachial plexus pattern  Eg. Musculocutaneous nerve = C5,6,7 / Ulnar nerve = C8, T1 Nerve Injury  Pressure, stretching (eg. Tumour) o Irritation  Altered sensation, pain, motor effects o Loss of function  Loss of sensation, weakness or paralysis  Crushing, tearing  Loss of function  May get partial or complete loss of function  May get motor loss and/or sensory loss  Sometimes the autonomic nervous system may be disturbed  Brachial plexus Injuries o Upper roots/trunk injuries  Head and shoulder pulled in opposite directions  Birth injuries  Falls (motorcycle accidents etc) o Lower roots/trunk injuries  Abduction and sudden stretch Arrested falls (hanging on to a support)  Birth injuries Musculocutaneous nerve Ulnar nerve Median nerve Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 25 Anatomy Lab 2: Axilla and Brachial Plexus Task 1: Boundaries of the Axilla  The axilla is the space between the thoracic wall and the upper part of the arm  Structures which pass between the limb and the neck or thorax pass through the axilla  Medial (ribs) and posterior (scapula) sides have bones. On the anterior side is the clavicle. Laterally the space narrows towards the humerus (anterior and posterior walls converge).  Apex of the axilla (cervico-axillary canal) is the gap bounded by the first rib, clavicle and the scapula  Boundaries of axilla: o Anterior Wall: Pectoralis major and minor o Posterior Wall: Latissimus dorsi and teres major o Medial Wall: Serratus anterior (covering the ribs)  Base is formed by skin and fascia that stretches between the three walls  The axilla contains major blood vessels (axillary artery and vein), part of the brachial plexus, fat and lymph nodes. o The axillary artery is the continuation of the subclavian artery which comes from the thorax through “the root of the neck” o The axillary artery have a part proximal the pectoralis minor, a part posterior, and a part distal (respectively 1st, 2nd, 3rd parts of the artery) o Brachial plexus is seen in the neck and the axilla Task 2: Brachial Plexus  Nerves forming brachial plexus are called “roots”, they are the ventral rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1  Roots and trunk are in the neck, divisions are behind the clavicle (when limb is in anatomical position), and the cords in the axilla. Beginnings of longer branches are also in the axilla.  Plexus is subject to significant variations  Branches form an “M” shape  5 Roots (C5-T1) form 3 Trunks (superior, middle, inferior)  Trunks have anterior and posterior division  Posterior divisions  Posterior cord (from all 3 posterior divisions)  Anterior Division  Lateral and medial cords (lateral cord forms from anterior divisions of superior and middle trunks. Medial cord forms from anterior divisions of inferior trunk  The 3 Cords are named by their positions around the 2nd part of the axillary artery (behind pectoralis minor). The posterior cord and nerves arising from it are therefore hidden by the artery. o Medial o Lateral Unite to form the median nerve  Form “M” shape Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 26 o Posterior  5 Branches from cords o Axilla o Radial o Ulnar – From medial cord o Musculocutaneous– from lateral cord o Median – Medial and Lat cords unite to form median nerve  Cutaneous nerves of the upper limb: Two of these (medial cutaneous nerves of the arm and forearm) arise from the medial cord itself. Medial cutaneous nerve of forearm may be mistaken for the ulnar nerve, note that the ulnar nerve is thicker, also look at the origin of the nerve to check. From posterior cord Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 27 Task 3: Osteology – Lower End of the Humerus, Upper Ends of Radius and Ulna  At lower end of humerus: o Capitulum, trochlear, medial and lateral epicondyles  Radius o Disc like upper end (“head”). Matches the capitulum of the humerus. o Little below head is radial tuberosity  Ulna o Upper end is large and irregular o Highest point, the olecranon, is posterior (point of the elbow) o Olecranon fits into the single large fossa on the posterior surface of the humerus o Upper end has a large notch called the trochlear notch, because it fits with the trochlear of the humerus. On the anterior side of this notch is a slight projection, the coronoid process, with a rough area just below. o In full flexion of the elbow, the radial head and the coronoid process of the ulna fit into the two small fossae in front. o The joint between he humerus and ulna has an extensive articular surface, it is the main joint of the elbow Task 4: Muscles in the Arm  Larger muscles in the arm act on the elbow joint  Parts of them may act on the shoulder joint (mainly serves as a stabilising mechanism)  Two compartments to logic with o Anterior: flexors of the elbow o Posterior: extensors of the elbow Anterior Compartment  All 3 muscles described below are supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve  Musculocutaneous can be Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 28 traced between the biceps and brachialis. The three vasti are extensors of the knee.  Rectus femoris is the straight muscle of the thigh. It originates from the anterior inferior iliac spine. It is an extensor of the knee and flexor of the hip.  Four parts merge into a common tendon which attaches to the tibial tuberosity  The patella is within the tendon Iliacus and Psoas Major  Iliacus is a broad muscle that arises from the iliac fossa and passes under the inguinal ligament  Psoas major is a cylindrical muscle that arises from the lumbar vertebrae and passes medial to the iliacus  The two muscles form a common tendon which attaches to the lesser trochanter. However the major part of the muscle is inserted into a band of deep fascia called the iliotibial band (tract). Gluteus Medius  The middle gluteal muscle, which attaches to the ilium between the posterior and anterior gluteal lines  It inserts distally to the greater trochanter Gluteus Minimus  The smallest gluteal muscle  Its proximal attachment is below the medius, between the anterior and inferior gluteal lines  It inserts to the greater trochanter Piriformis and Sciatic Nerve  Originates from the anterior (inner) surface of the sacrum and emerges through the greater sciatic foramen  It a very important landmark muscle because emerging from the pelvis below it, is the sciatic nerve  A little below and deep to the sciatic nerve are three muscles the larger obturator internus, and two gemelli above and below. Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 67 Task 4: The Foot Bones  Calcaneum: Joins the talus in front via a complex articular surface, has a prominent tuberosity at the heel  Talus: Forms the ankle joint, has a rounded front end (head) which fits with the curved navicular. It is supported by a shelf like projection from the calcaneum called the sustentaculum tali.  Navicular  Cuboid  Cuneiform (x3): On the lateral side of the cuboid  A complex system of ligaments holds the bones. Functionally the most important of these is the spring ligament (plantar calcaneonavicular ligament)  The ankle joint has strong medial (deltoid) and lateral ligaments  The surface of the talus forming the ankle joint is narrow posteriorly, the fibrous joint between the tibia and fibula accommodates this surface during dorsiflexion and plantarflexion Muscles  General arrangement of structures is similar to that in the hand  However the foot does not have the fine movements of the hand. The muscles serve mainly to maintain the bony architecture and adjustments during walking.  Muscles of the great toe and little toe parallel those of the thenar and hypothenar eminence  Interossei and lumbricals have a similar pattern except that the “axis” passes through the second toe  The large adductor hallucis is similar to the adductor pollicis  Major differences between the foot and hand are: o The Flexor digitorum longus is comparable to the flexor digitorum profundus of the upper limb, attaching to distal phalanges o The flexor digitorum brevis is comparable to the flexor digitorum superficialis o The long flexor tendons enter from the medial side of the foot, with an oblique pull on the more lateral digits. This is corrected by an extra muscle called the flexor accessories (also called the quadratus plantae)  The nerves of the foot are the medial and lateral plantar nerves, both branches of the tibial. The adductors (largely coming from the ischiopubic ramus) are medial rotators.  Theoretically a muscle that pulls the femur forward is a medial rotator  Theoretically a muscle that pulls the femur backwards is a lateral rotator Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 70 Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 71 Task 2: The Knee Joint  There is a disparity in size between the condyles of the femur and tibia  The femoral condyles also have a different degree of curvature to the tibial surface which must adjust their shape. This is done by the menisci.  In movement of the joint the rolling of the femoral condyles is accompanied by sliding so the bones are able to maintain contact with each other  This motion is assisted by the cruciate ligaments  In flexion to extension, the movements are completed by the smaller lateral condyles first, and then the last bit of movement involves medial rotation of the femur  The joint has two sets of independent articular surfaces (medial and lateral)  The cruciate ligaments form an incomplete partition between the lateral and medial parts of the joint  Functionally the knee is a hinge joint, however in a semi flexed knee the leg can be rotated medially and laterally to a small extent  Anteriorly the capsule of the joint is attached all around the patella, and is strengthened by the quadriceps tendon and patella ligament (patella tendon). Fibrous extensions from the vasti add further strength.  On the sides the capsule is strengthened by the collateral ligaments, tibial (medial) and fibular (lateral)  Menisci are fibrocartilaginous structures which deepen the tibial condyles. They are attached to the fibrous capsule. They move slightly with movements of the knee joint.  Cruciate ligaments (anterior and posterior) are named by their attachments to the tibia Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 72 Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 73 Task 3: Regional Anatomy and Revision Femoral Triangle  Boundaries: Inguinal ligament, Sartorius and adductor longus  The femoral vessels and nerve run through the femoral triangle  Medial to the femoral vein is the femoral canal (a fat filled space). The upper limit of the canal is the femoral ring, bounded medially by the lacunar ligament  The femoral sheath is formed by fascial layers from the abdominal walls  Posterior to the femoral neurovascular bundle is the pectineus muscle  Close to the lateral end of the inguinal ligament, the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh can be seen. It passage through or behind the ligament is a common site of entrapment.  Below the apex of the femoral triangle, the vessels pass through a fascial canal deep to the Sartorius muscle (the subsartorial/adductor/ Hunter’s canal) Nerves and Their Muscle Groups  Obturator nerve goes to the adductor muscles  The sciatic nerve emerges below the piriformis muscle. It bifurcates (normally in the popliteal fossa) into the tibial and common fibular nerves.  The tibial nerve disappears deep to the soleus muscle and supplies the muscles in the posterior compartment of the leg. It is then seen on the medial side of the ankle.  The common fibular nerve winds around the neck of the fibula (it is commonly injured at this location with fractures of the bone) and divides into superficial and deep branches. The superficial fibular supplies the lateral compartment muscles and the deep branch supplies the anterior compartment muscles. Neuromuscular Junction 1. Action potential propagates down axon of cell to terminal button (saltatory conduction) 2. Activation of voltage gated Ca2+ channels, calcium influx from cytoplasm to terminal button 3. Presence of calcium allows vesicles (containing a neurotransmitter eg. acetylcholine) to fuse with the membrane. By exocytosis the neurotransmitter enters the synaptic cleft where it can diffuse across to the motor end plate. 4. Neurotransmitter binds to acetylcholine (ligand) receptors, causing them to open, which allows the influx of sodium and the outflux of potassium 5. This causes and excitatory post synaptic potential (EPSP, -70mV), which causes opening of voltage gated Na channels on the muscle membrane and then action potential propagation in the muscle fibre  The density of acetylcholine receptors is very high. This is important because lots of the acetylcholine released is rapidly broken down by acetylcholinesterase into acetate and choline (which may be recycled to produce more acetylcholine). The high density means there is enough acetylcholine to generate a sufficient EPSP to generate an action potential  Effective communication from motoneuron to muscle. Disturbances to skeletal muscle function and to the neural circuits that control skeletal muscles give rise to a variety of derangements of movement that are important clinically. Skeletal Muscle Organisation and the Motor Neuron Pool  Muscle is activated by action potentials travelling down the axons of the alpha motor neurons  The alpha motor neurons that innervate a muscle are referred to as that muscle’s “motor neuron pool”. For the limbs and axial muscles, the cell bodies of the motor neurons lie in the ventral horn of the spinal cord. The motor axons exit through the ventral roots.  Motor neurons receive both excitatory and inhibitory synaptic input from a variety of pathways: o Descending pathways from higher centres involved in voluntary movement and postural control o Sensory neurons whose receptive endings lie in the muscles, tendons, skin and joints. The cell bodies of these neurons lie in the dorsal root ganglia and their central processes enter the spinal cord through the dorsal roots.  Each motor neuron innervates a number of muscle fibres within its target muscle. The motor neuron and the group of muscle fibres to which it is connected are termed a “motor unit” Learning Outcomes  Describe motor unit organisation and size variation of motor units  Explain the pattern of recruitment of motor units and the relation to force generation by muscle  Describe the technique of electromyography and what it measures  Describe and explain the relationship between EMG recordings seen during voluntary contraction and the motor unit organisation of muscle  Describe the different modes of activation and inhibition of flexor and extensor muscles  Debate the possible mechanisms and site of muscle fatigue  Measure muscle responses to motor nerve stimulation and calculate conduction velocity in a motor nerve  Explain the reflex circuits that regulate muscle contraction – in particular, tendon reflexes and polysynaptic cutaneous reflexes Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 124  Each motor unit behaves as a functional distinct and discrete entity. In healthy muscle, the excitatory synapse between the motor neuron and each muscle fibre is an extremely secure and efficient one, and each presynaptic action potential that arrives at the motor endplate ultimately gives rise to a postsynaptic muscle action potential. All muscle fibres in a motor unit therefore discharge together, generating a single summed action potential seen in an extracellular recording, and termed a “motor unit potential”.  The technique of extracellular recording of motor unit potentials is called “electromyography”.  Each motor unit contributes an increment of force to the overall force generated by the muscle.  Motor units vary in size (number of muscle fibres that the motor neuron innervates) within any given muscle and the average motor unit size varies in different types of muscles. The size of the motor unit action potential and the force increment vary roughly proportionally with the size of the motor units.  When the strength of excitation of the motor neuron pool is increased, small motor units are fired off first. For stronger and stronger excitation more and larger motor units are “recruited” into the active population. The order of recruitment is fixed ensuring that small motor units are always used for small forces. For maximum force generation, all motor units in a muscle are activated.  The total force generated by a muscle depends on the number and size of the motor units that are active. For a steady level of force output, the firing of each motor unit is asynchronous with respect to other motor units and hence the total force is the smoothed sum of the individual contributions. Force is also regulated to some extent by the firing rate within each motor unit, since this also results in more or less summation of force. Functional Organization of Muscles around a Joint  Muscles that operate to control the angle of a joint are generally arranged in opposing sets of flexors and extensors. The biceps for example, when it contracts unopposed, causes flexion of the forearm, whereas the triceps causes extension.  The nervous system has various strategies for controlling the contraction of flexors and extensors in a co-ordinated manner. The pattern of contraction of flexors and extensors controlling a joint varies according to the task. For example: o When flexing or extending a limb voluntarily with a minimal load, one muscle group is commanded to contract, and the other is relaxed. This is achieved by the descending voluntary pathways simultaneously exciting one motor neuron pool and inhibiting the other. The inhibition is achieved by activating inhibitory interneurons. o In contrast, when there is a need to stabilize the angle of a joint, for example in resisting an external force that could cause unwanted limb movement, the flexors and extensors are activated together. This is achieved by descending voluntary pathways acting on different interneuron groups within the spinal cord so as to simultaneously increase the level of excitation of both groups of motor neurons. Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 125 Reflex Control of Muscle  Peripheral sensory neurons can have a variety of effects on the motor neuron pool and hence on the state of muscle contraction. These influences: o Generate reflex responses to discrete stimuli o Have profound effects on the steady state of contraction of muscle (the so called “muscle tone”) o Determine the response of the muscle to imposed stretch or rises in tension.  There are different kinds of reflexes that can be commonly observed: o Tendon reflexes (also known as T-reflexes, stretch reflexes, myotactic reflexes): In this reflex, the tap to the tendon imparts a stretch on the muscle. It is this length change that is sensed by the muscle spindle sensory neuron (the Ia primary afferent) eventually causing a reflex excitation of the motor neurons, opposing the stretch by active contraction of the muscle. The tonic activity in this reflex arc determines the level of muscle tone and the sensitivity of the reflex determines the resistance of the muscle to imposed stretch. The reflex works automatically to regulate muscle length in large postural muscles and so helps to automatically maintain posture. o The “inverse myoactic reflex”: Unlike the previous tendon reflex, this reflex occurs in response to large rises in muscle tension, not length changes per se. The sensory neuron is a different one from the muscles spindle afferent neuron, and it is located in the muscle tendon, not in the muscle itself. The circuit, when activated, causes a reflex inhibition, not excitation, of the motor neurons, and hence a sudden relaxation of the muscle when tension rises (the so-called “clasp-knife reflex”). This reflex was thought to serve a protective function, reducing the likelihood of muscle and tendon tearing. However, it is now known that the reflex operates even at very small increments of muscle force and serves as vital negative feedback mechanism for controlling muscle output during, for example, grasping of delicate objects. Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 126 o Polysynaptic cutaneous reflexes: Cutaneous sensory neurons provide complex synaptic inputs to the motor neuron pools. The circuit shown is that of the well known “crossed extensor reflex” that mediates limb withdrawal from a painful stimulus, while through a set of reciprocal excitatory and inhibitory connections to the motoneuron pool on the opposite side of the spinal cord, it results in automatic extension of the other limb. . Variability of Reflexes  Reflexes can be altered by lesions  Any lesion in the afferent or efferent parts of the reflex circuit will affect the strength of the reflex that can be elicited. Hence, damage to dorsal or ventral roots, or degeneration of afferent or motor neurons will result in hyporeflexia. Such lesions will also cause a reduction in muscle tone and resistance to stretch.  Spinal reflexes are subject to constant modulation by important descending neural pathways. These pathways can exert effects either on the alpha motor neurons, or on the small gamma motor neurons that act to regulate the sensitivity of the muscle spindles. Hence, lesions to these pathways can lead to either hyperreflexia or hyporeflexia and also to changes in muscle tone and resistance to stretch.  Polysynaptic cutaneous reflexes are complex in their neural circuitry and also receive modulatory descending inputs. Hence, such reflexes are sometimes changed to a different pattern when there are higher centre lesions. Experiment 1: Observations of the Motor Unit Activity of a Working Muscle  The electrical events observed are motor unit potentials, which comprise of all the muscle fibres discharging (due to individual action potentials) together and summating  As the muscle force increases the amplitude of electrical activity also increases. This is because more motor units are recruited, in order to generate more force. Experiment 2: Location of Normal Muscle Fatigue  In the neuromuscular system fatigue could be the result of: o Failure of force generation within muscle o Failure of neuromuscular transmission o Failure of motor neuron firing o Failure of motor commands between cortex and motor neurons  As fatigue progresses the overall level of EMG activity increases. This suggests the cellular locus of fatigue is within the muscle fibres themself. The subject is able to maintain the same limb position, even though the muscle fatigues, by the recruitment of more motor units, in order to maintain the required force. This is due to the fact that different groups of interneurons are activated. This also happens between two different parts of the same body Torque  For a point mass, equilibrium (no acc.) requires all forces to balance (taking directions into account)  For an extended body another condition must be considered  For F1 and F2 to be in equilibrium we must have F1D1=F2D2  In general F x D is called the torque of force F, where D is the perpendicular distance from F to the point  If forces F1 and F2 are in equilibrium and if not energy is dissipated as they rotate the body about the pivot point B then the work done by one force is equal to the work done on the other  For an extended rigid body to be in equilibrium i. Forces in x, y, z directions must balance ii. Torques about x, y, z axes must balance Examples of Equilibrium  Consider a simple beam pivoted at B with forces F1 and F2 acting at each end and F3 acting at the pivot point. These apply torques about any point on a vertical line shown  If these torques are to balance we must have: ∑j dm g aj= ∑i dm g ai  Where dm is the magnitude of each element of mass  Hence: ∑j aj= ∑i ai  This defines a line which includes the centre of gravity  The same is done in 3 mutually perpendicular directions to define a point, which is the centre of gravity Centre of Mass  The above analysis also works for accelerations of the body (not just gravitational forces)  If a mass is accelerated, forces are required on each element of the mass. If the total force is applied through the centre of gravity the force will have no tendency to rotate the body.  Thus centre of gravity is the centre of mass, irrespective of existence of a gravitational field Free Motion of an Extended Body  A body freely moving can be assumed to have 2 independent motions: o Rotation about centre of mass. In the absence of torques the angular momentum will remain constant. o Motion of the centre of mass as if the body were a point mass. In a gravitational field the path will be parabolic. Centre of Mass of the Body  For a person standing erect with good posture the centre of mass is approximately given by: o In the anterior plane: centre of mass is approximately waist level o In the sagittal plane, a vertical line of centre of mass goes through:  Centre of mass of skull  Centre of rib cage  Centre of pelvis  Axis of femur  Ball of foot in front of ankle  The centre of mass is usually in front of the spinal column. This means that if we consider the upper body as being supported by the spine at its point of attachment to the pelvis then the weight of the body creates a torque about this point of attachment. Equilibrium can only occur if there is an equal and opposite torque elsewhere. A large part of this torque arises from muscular force applied by the back muscles (erector spinae). Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 153  The centre of mass must always be over the point of support because if not the body will rotate about the point of support since the supporting force and the force of gravity (person’s weight) will exert a torque around any chosen point. The diagram shows that if not, the body’s weight applies a torque about the point of support. This will cause the body to rotate (fall over).  The position of the centre of mass can be obtained by measuring the line of action of the weight (mg a = F b) Effect of Posture  Centre of mass is not fixed, it is determined by the distribution of mass  It is possible to have centre of mass outside the body (eg. by bending over)  Position of centre of mass of two bodies is found on a line joining the centre of mass’ and a distance proportional to the two masses from each  Hence for M and m separated by d, centre of mass is on the line joining masses and a distance [m/(M+m)] d, from mass M or [M/(M+m)] d from mass m.  Changes in weight distribution require change in posture to retain the centre of mass over the feet Balance  Our upright stance is inherently unstable. We need to continually adjust the position of the centre of mass to keep it over the feet. There is a complex feedback system which relies on the eyes, the inner ear and the muscles. Hence centre of mass is continually moving.  You can also retain balance by moving parts of your body (eg. arms, legs) Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 154 Lecture 17: Forces Applied to the Body  The body can move some of its component parts with respect to other parts. This is done by contracting muscles. These are attached to bones via tendons. The attachment points allow torques to be applied which cause rotation of the body components.  The attachment point of the muscles is crucial in determining the type and amount of movement possible and also in controlling the forces that the body can apply to external objects for given muscle strength.  The attachments are at positions such that the efficiency of the muscle is in general poor, which means that the muscles exert substantially greater forces than can be applied to external objects. However, the advantage of this is that small amounts of muscular contraction can produce significant movement. The Forearm  The forces due to the weight of the forearm and the mass being carried are balanced by the tension applied to the forearm by the biceps  If these were the only forces applied to the forearm it could not be in equilibrium. There has to be another anticlockwise torque. This is supplied by a reaction force between upper and lower arms which occurs at the elbow. This force must also be vertical and in a downward direction on the forearm.  In lifting 20.4 kg the tension force is sufficient to lift 160 kg. This is a very inefficient lever, however it allows you to lift objects through a large distance Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 155 Upper Arm  If we consider the forces on the upper arm it can be seen that the upper arm will rotate unless it receives an anti-clockwise torque. This must be supplied at the shoulder joint. A simple vertical force at the shoulder cannot balance the downward T and the upward R forces which will occur at the elbow. Head Support The Foot Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 156  If a person stands on one foot on the rung of a ladder in such a manner that the person is in equilibrium and with no other forces applied to the body, the rung must apply an upward force equal to the weight of the body (consider the body overall).  The centre of mass of the body will be directly above the rung  The foot now has an upward force applied by the rung and a downward force applied to the foot by the leg  With no other forces applied, the foot will rotate, since the torques do not balance.  The foot may be kept in balance either by application of a downward force on the toe or an upward force on the heel. The latter is in fact how the foot is prevented from rotating. The calf muscles contract to apply a force to the Achilles tendon which is applied about 5 cm behind the ankle joint.  If the rail is 20 cm in front of the ankle joint: T x 0.05 = 700 x0.20 (torques about ankle) where 700 N is the weight of the body  Thus, T = 2800 N and R = 3500 N so that the force applied by the leg on the foot is 5 times the person's weight The Back  Forces on the spine must exist even when standing (or forces applied to the body sitting) with the whole body in equilibrium. Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 157 Lecture 20: Friction, Lubrication, Joints Friction Force  In principle, any force no matter how small will produce acceleration. Also a moving body will continue to move in a straight line (at a constant velocity, including a stationary body) unless acted upon by a force. However in practice all bodies that we are familiar with will slow down. This is due to the force of friction.  Friction is a force that opposes motion  If a force is applied to a stationary body does not cause it to accelerate, the frictional force is equal and opposite to an applied force on a body, so that the total force is 0 Rules for Friction 1. The frictional force, F, is proportional to “normal reaction” N F=μN 2. μ is the coefficient of friction. Static friction is usually larger than kinetic friction. 3. μ depends on the state of the two surfaces and the material of which they are made  μ generally has values between 0.1 and 1.5 The Importance of Friction  Friction is crucial to the mechanical function of our bodies. For example friction is necessary in walking as it opposes the muscular forces we apply to the ground through our feet, with the reaction force propelling us forward. It is impossible to walk normally if μ is less than about 0.1.  We also rely on friction for most other activities such as standing and picking up objects. However there are also situations in which we want to reduce friction as much as possible, such as in the joints, where bones need to move over one another. Work to Overcome Friction  In the knee joint bone surfaces slide over each other as a person walks. We assume that the surfaces slide a distance of 5cm with each stride, that the normal reaction is 700N (in reality due to dynamic effects this may be 2 to 3 times higher), and the stride rate is 1 per second.  Friction Force = μ x 700 N  Work done = μ x 700 x 0.05 = μ x 35 Joule  Power = μ x 35 Watts. Since total power liberation by the body is about 100 Watts, clearly μ must be small. For a body joint μ is approximately 0.03 (very low). This is achieved by the use of very smooth surfaces, and lubrication, of the joint surfaces. Lubrication  For smooth surfaces friction mainly arises due to molecular forces between atoms which are in intimate contact. For most surfaces the contact only occurs at very few places due to undulations in the surface on the microscopic scale.  Lubrication normally involves placing a material between the surfaces which prevents this intimate contact.  A good lubricant must: o Have lubricant layer thicker than the irregularities (asperities) of the surface. This means it is easier to lubricate a smooth surface, otherwise the lubricating layer needs to Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 158 be thicker. Typically the surfaces need to be smooth to about 1-50 μm (wavelength of light = 0.5 μm). o Exhibit weak interaction between its own atoms. I.e. it must be able to bond to the surface molecules well, but not to its own molecules well. This means the lubricant molecules end up sliding over each other, not the molecules of the two surfaces.  Methods used to keep lubricant between the surfaces include: 1. Hydrostatic lubrication: Hydrostatic pressure is used to keep lubricant between the surfaces (eg. In a car motor) 2. Hydrodynamic lubrication: Dynamic action keeps lubricant between the surfaces (eg. Rotating bearings). This is also relevant to human’s joints where lubricant may be forced into joints as they move. 3. Squeeze film: A viscous lubricant which is not squeezed from between the surfaces when pressure is applied (eg. graphite used as a solid lubricant in engineering). Some lubricant will be excluded and this must be restored when the load is removed. Knee Joint  The femur and tibia slide over each other and hence removal of friction is essential.  To avoid friction at the joint and reduce the force necessary to rotate the lower leg, the bone surfaces are covered with cartilage, and the joint is encased in a capsule which is lined by a synovial membrane, which secretes synovial fluid into the joint for lubrication  Cartilage: o Cartilage is a collagen combined with a hydrated proteoglycan gel. It is slightly deformable and permeable. This means under instantaneous force it acts as an elastic material and is able to cushion the effects of sudden forces. o Collagen fibres aligned in arcades, making them perpendicular to bone at the bone surface and parallel at the cartilage surface. The perpendicular arrangement at depth helps to anchor the cartilage to the bone, and the surface arrangement makes the surface smooth to 2 to 5 μm.  Synovial Fluid: o A dialysate of blood plasma containing protein molecules (eg. hyaluronic acid of MW approximately 106) o It has a high viscosity, which increases as it becomes more concentrated o The synovial fluid acts as a boundary lubricant with both cartilage surfaces moving freely over each other due to the lubricating effects of the synovial fluid. When pressure is applied to a joint, the synovial fluid is squeezed from between the two surfaces. o Under pressure for short periods (low pressures and fast motion), there is not time to squeeze the lubricant out of the joint (in fact the pressure may squeeze the lubricant into the region ahead of the region of greatest pressure, thereby allowing its lubrication as surfaces pass over one another). Normal Systems: Musculoskeletal System Notes Semester 2, 2010 Toby Dawson (20517564) Page 159 o Under pressure for long periods, it is essential that not all of the synovial fluid is squeezed from between the surfaces. It is believed that there are two mechanisms that maintain lubrication. The first is that pressure applied to the cartilage has a tendency to squeeze synovial fluid from it and therefore replenish the lubricant. It is also believed that the synovial fluid becomes even more viscous due to the loss of lower molecular weight molecules preferentially, while higher molecular weight molecules (hyaluronic acid) remain. This causes the viscosity to increase so that it becomes more difficult to exclude the synovial fluid and there is always some lubricant between the cartilage surfaces. Hence the synovial fluid will remain between the surfaces even under pressure for a long period of time. Degenerative Joint Disease  Many joint diseases such as osteoarthritis result in changes to the joint which reduce its effectiveness. Such changes include: o Roughening of the cartilage o Absence of cartilage o Thickening or deformation of the bone  These changes dramatically modify the ease with which the joint can operate and cause pain during its movement
